summary: DHH draws a parallel between Disney's plan to cut animation costs by completing scripts before production begins and the software industry's perennial belief that better upfront planning will solve cost and schedule problems. He references Fred Brooks' 'No Silver Bullet' essay to suggest this approach is naive. The post implies that creative production—whether animation or software—is inherently iterative and resistant to waterfall-style planning.
key_points: Disney's Vanguard partnership planned to halve animation costs by finishing scripts before production starts
DHH sees this as the same flawed logic that plagues software development: the belief that more upfront planning eliminates downstream problems
References Brooks' 'No Silver Bullet' to argue that no single process change can deliver dramatic productivity gains in complex creative work
money_quotes: Why does this sound familiar? There is no silver bullet.
key_insight: The belief that completing all planning before production begins will dramatically cut costs is a recurring fallacy in both animation and software development—there is no silver bullet.
